description: 100bb deep, BTN vs BB. You (BTN) opened to 2.5bb, BB 3-bet to 11bb, you called. Flop is Kh 7d 2s. BB checks to you. The pot is 22.5bb.
gto_explanation: When the 3-bettor checks a high-equity board like K72 rainbow into the in-position cold-caller, the BB's range is heavily capped (they would c-bet most of their KK/AA/AK for protection and value). The BTN cold-caller has a range disadvantage on this board because BB has more Kx in their 3-bet range. Solvers strongly favor a high check-back frequency here to protect the BTN's range and to realize equity with weak pairs and backdoor draws.

description: 100bb deep, full ring, folded to CO. CO opens 2.5bb. Hero is in the BB with A5 offsuit. Action is on Hero.
gto_explanation: A5o is at the bottom of BB's defending range but is a high-frequency 3-bet bluff candidate, not a flat. The blocker to AA/AK and the wheel-straight potential make it block more value than a hand like K5o, and 3-betting recovers fold equity that calling out of position cannot. Flatting OOP with A5o realizes equity poorly. Solvers 3-bet A5o in BB-vs-CO at high frequency.

description: 100bb. SRP. SB completed, BTN raised to 3bb, SB called. Flop 9c 6c 2h: SB check, BTN bet 3bb, SB call. Turn 9c 6c 2h Tc (flush completing): SB check. Pot 14bb, stacks 87bb. Action on BTN.
gto_explanation: The turned third club is a brutal scare card for BTN's range — BTN c-bet flop with a polarized range that includes many one-pair hands but few flush combinations relative to SB, who can have all the small suited connectors that flop a draw and got there. BTN's pure value (sets, two pair) is now drawn out on and BTN's bluffs lose equity against the calling range's many made flushes. Solvers strongly favor checking back most of BTN's range on this turn including most one-pair hands, betting only the nut-flush and bluffs containing the Ac blocker.

description: 40bb effective. UTG (Hero) at a 6-max table with 22. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: At 40bb effective from UTG in 6-max, small pocket pairs like 22-44 no longer have the set-mining odds they need against the depth-adjusted calling/3-betting ranges. Implied odds shrink because if you flop a set against a 3-bettor at 40bb, you cannot get a full 'set value' stacked off the cooler. The mixed-strategy range tightens at this depth and 22 falls below the cutoff for UTG opens. This is a 'shallow-depth UTG fold' that players who open standard 100bb ranges leak with.

description: 100bb. SB raised to 3bb, BB called. Flop Qh 7s 4d: SB bet 2bb, BB called. Turn Qh 7s 4d 8s: SB check, BB check. River Qh 7s 4d 8s 3h: BB check. Hero (SB) has KQ offsuit. Pot 10bb. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: KQ is top pair top kicker with strong showdown value, but on a river where BB checks twice on a low-action runout BB's range is full of Qx with worse kickers (Q9, QT, QJ at frequency), middle pairs, and busted spade draws. A small thin-value bet around 30% pot extracts value from worse Qx and weak middle pairs (8x, 7x) that will call once but never twice, while keeping the bluff range balanced. Over-betting bloats the pot against a range that has very little KQ+ left after the BB check-called flop and checked turn.

description: 100bb. CO opens 2.5bb, BTN (Hero) calls. Flop 8h 8d 3c: CO bets 5bb (~75% pot) into 6.5bb. Hero has 9d 9s. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: 99 is an overpair to the board but the texture is dynamic against CO's polar c-betting range. Raising blows out the small pairs and weak overcards CO is bluff-c-betting, which is the part of CO's range we beat. Folding is far too tight — we beat all bluffs and many of CO's middling pairs. Calling keeps CO's bluffs in and lets us reassess on the turn against a polarized barreling range. Solvers mostly call 99 here.
lesson_tag: Call don't raise vs polar c-bet

description: 100bb. UTG opens 2.5bb, BTN calls. Flop Tc 9c 5d UTG bet 4bb, BTN call. Turn Tc 9c 5d 2h: UTG bet 11bb, BTN call. River Tc 9c 5d 2h 6s: UTG bets 50bb (~125% pot overbet). Hero (BTN) has Td Jd. Pot was 39bb. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: TJ on this runout is a marginal bluff-catcher with top pair decent kicker, but UTG's triple-barrel overbet line on a relatively dry river polarizes to sets, two pair, JJ+, and rivered straights (87, 8c7c missing some), with bluffs primarily missed flush draws. The minimum-defense frequency against an overbet of this size demands continuing only the strongest bluff-catchers; TJ blocks none of UTG's value combos (no straight blocker, no flush blocker since no club). Solvers fold TJ at high frequency in this exact spot.
lesson_tag: MDF and blockers vs polar river overbet

description: 100bb. Hero opens 2.5bb from CO with 55. BTN 3-bets to 9bb. Action back on Hero.
gto_explanation: 55 has clean set-mining odds against a 3-bet to 9bb when 100bb deep: paying 6.5bb to win a pot that can become ~200bb when you flop a set roughly 1 in 8.5 times gives the implied odds needed. 4-betting bloats the pot with a hand that has poor equity against the 3-betting continue range and that performs poorly post-flop without a set. Folding is too tight in position with set-mining odds and a hand that can still cooler aces.

description: 100bb. HJ opens 2.5bb. BB (Hero) calls with KdJd. Flop Kc 5h 4s: BB check, HJ bet 3bb, BB call. Turn Kc 5h 4s 7c: BB check, HJ bet 9bb. Hero must decide.
gto_explanation: KJ is top pair with a decent kicker. Folding to a turn double barrel sized at ~70% pot when we beat all of HJ's bluffs and many of their thinner value bets (KT, K9 in some constructions, JJ-99) is far too tight. Check-raising turns our hand into a bluff and removes bluffs we beat from HJ's range. Solvers call KJ at very high frequency on K54r-7 here — it is precisely the type of hand the line is designed to defend.

description: 100bb. CO opens 2.5bb. BB (Hero) calls. Flop 7c 6c 5d. Pot is 5.5bb. Hero has 8h 4h. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: On low connected boards like 765 the BB has a range advantage relative to the CO opener because BB calls all the small connectors (87, 76s, 65s, 54s, 87s for some constructions) that the CO does not open. Solvers have begun donking these boards at meaningful frequencies with the nutted part of the range (made straights) plus bluffs that have wheel equity. 84o is a strong donking bluff candidate here — it has clean equity to backdoor straight, blocks 87, and turns into a barrel target. Solvers donk lead BB on 765-type boards at ~25-40% frequency.

description: 12bb stack, MTT bubble. Hero (BTN) holds A8 offsuit. Folded to Hero. Blinds 1bb/2bb.
gto_explanation: At 12bb effective from the BTN A8 offsuit is a clear push in Nash open-shove tables. Min-raising at this depth creates terrible play decisions — you can't fold to a 3-bet shove but you put yourself in marginal flips for half your stack, and you let the blinds realize their equity. Shoving denies that equity and forces the blinds to call only with hands that dominate A8 or coin-flip with it. Folding A8o at 12bb on the BTN is a major Nash leak.

description: 100bb. SRP. BTN (Hero) opens 2.5bb, BB calls. Flop 9h 8h 2c: BB check, BTN bet 2bb, BB call. Turn 9h 8h 2c Jh (third heart): BB check, BTN check. River 9h 8h 2c Jh 4d: BB bets 8bb into a 9.5bb pot (~80%). Hero has AhKc.
gto_explanation: AhKc has the nut flush blocker and no showdown value otherwise. BB's range on this river contains made flushes, J-high pairs, two pair (84, 92 etc that flatted preflop), and some bluffs (busted gutshots that picked up the third heart and decided to bluff). The Ah blocker is exactly the card we want — we remove a big chunk of BB's flush combinations, making the bluff-to-value ratio much more favorable for a call. Folding is too tight given the price and the blocker; raising loses to all flushes BB has.

description: 100bb cash. UTG limps 1bb. Folds to BTN (Hero) with 89 suited.
gto_explanation: 89 suited is a strong isolating hand against a UTG limper because it makes nutted hands (straights, flushes) when it flops well, plays well IP heads-up against a capped limping range, and discourages the blinds from coming along cheaply. Limping behind invites multiway pots which dilute equity for a hand like 89s. Folding is far too tight on the button against a limper. The isolation size is bigger than a standard open (5bb instead of 2.5bb) to charge the limper for being out of position.

description: 100bb cash. Hero opens HJ to 2.5bb with A5 suited. BTN 3-bets to 9bb. Folds back to Hero.
gto_explanation: A5s is one of the canonical 4-bet bluff candidates because of the ace blocker (removes AA/AK from BTN's continue range) and the wheel/flush potential when called. Flatting OOP with A5s realizes equity poorly. Folding gives up too much equity. 4-betting recovers fold equity, polarizes our range correctly, and makes a strong made hand when called. Solvers 4-bet bluff A5s at high frequency in HJ vs BTN 3-bet.

description: 100bb. CO opens 2.5bb, BTN (Hero) calls. Flop Js 8c 3d: CO bet 3bb, BTN call. Turn Js 8c 3d 4h: CO check, BTN bet 5bb, CO call. River Js 8c 3d 4h 2s: CO checks. Hero has 7d 7c. Pot is 19bb.
gto_explanation: 77 has showdown value: we beat 88-... wait, we lose to 88. We beat 66, 55, weak 8x that BTN bet for thin value would not include here, and busted draws. CO's calling range on the turn likely contains Jx and middle pairs that beat us; betting for value gets called only by hands that beat us. Bluffing makes no sense because we have showdown value. Check back to realize the showdown. A common mistake is to 'put pressure' here when the right action is to take the cheap showdown.

description: 100bb. BTN (Hero) opens 2.5bb with AcQc. BB calls. Flop 8c 5c 2h. BB checks. Pot 5.5bb.
gto_explanation: AcQc has the nut flush draw plus two overcards, with about 50% equity against BB's calling range. The small (~35% pot) c-bet captures BB's many missing-equity hands (the entire underpair-low-card range that called preflop), denies equity to overcards that beat us, and sets up a high-leverage turn semibluff if we hit our draw or fire again. Overbetting is unnecessary because BB's calling range is wide and merged on this dry-looking board; bigger sizes lose value from hands that fold to a small bet anyway.

description: 100bb. CO (Hero) opens 2.5bb with QQ. BTN calls. Flop 7h 4s 2d: CO bet 2bb, BTN call. Turn 7h 4s 2d 5s: CO bet 6bb, BTN call. River 7h 4s 2d 5s 6c (4-card straight on board): CO bet 10bb, BTN raises to 35bb. Hero must decide. Stacks left ~80bb each.
gto_explanation: When a 4-card straight completes on the river and a flat-calling station finally check-raises, almost every combination of value they could have here is now a straight (8x, 3x). QQ blocks no straights and we have no equity against the straight portion of the range. The only hands BTN could be raising for value that we beat would be uncharacteristic spazz-bluffs; the calling line indicates BTN had a hand all along. Solvers fold even overpairs to a c/r in this exact texture at high frequency.

description: 100bb. Hero opens BTN 2.5bb with 66. BB calls. Flop 6h 9c 2s. BB checks, Hero bets 2bb, BB raises to 8bb. Hero is on the BTN with bottom set.
gto_explanation: Bottom set on a relatively dry board against a check-raise is the canonical 'build the pot now' spot. BB's check-raise range includes 99 (top set), 22 (bottom set 2x), 9-6 two pair, semi-bluffs with backdoor equity, and some bluffs. We are far ahead of everything except 99 and we want to get the money in while BB's range still contains all the worse hands. Flatting risks the turn bringing a scare card that lets BB pot-control with us when we want to be punishing BB's whole range. Three-betting denies equity to draws and gets called by worse made hands.
lesson_tag: Set on dry board: bet for value

description: 100bb. BTN (Hero) opens with 5c 4c. BB calls. Flop Jh 7c 3c: BB check, BTN bet 2bb, BB call. Turn Jh 7c 3c Td: BB check, BTN check. River Jh 7c 3c Td 2s: BB checks. Pot 9bb. Hero has 5c 4c (missed flush, missed straight, busted).
gto_explanation: We have zero showdown value (5-high) and the turn check has capped BB's range — they would have led most of their stronger Jx and Tx hands by the river or check-raised the turn. The river card is a brick. A medium-sized bluff exploits the capped check-check line: BB's range is predominantly weak Jx, busted draws, and low pairs, all of which fold to a polarized river bet. Checking back gives up the pot with the worst hand. Over-betting is unnecessary because we don't need to fold out the top of BB's range — there is no top.

description: 100bb. UTG limps 1bb. MP raises to 4bb. Folds back to UTG (Hero) who has AA. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: The 'limp-reraise' is a legitimate balanced line at 100bb depth precisely because the limping range needs to include a few monsters to prevent opponents from blasting limpers off their hand profitably. Flatting AA after limping caps your range and invites multiway pots where AA performs poorly. The reraise size is roughly 3.5x the isolation to a similar effective number as a standard 3-bet. Folding AA is obviously catastrophic. Calling traps you in a multiway-prone pot.

description: 100bb. SB 3-bets Hero's BTN open, Hero (BTN) 4-bets to 21bb, SB calls. Flop Kd 7s 4h. Pot ~43bb with ~79bb behind (SPR ~1.8). SB checks. Hero holds Ac Qd.
gto_explanation: In 4-bet pots the SPR is so low that the 4-bettor keeps a large range-wide equity edge on most flops. On K74 rainbow Hero's 4-bet range (KK+, AK, plus AQ/A5s-type blends) crushes SB's flat-calling range, so Hero can c-bet the entire range for a tiny ~15-20% size. AQ specifically has two overcards and a backdoor draw and wants to deny equity cheaply while setting up a stacks-in turn. Larger sizes are unnecessary when the whole range bets and the low SPR guarantees commitment anyway.

description: 100bb. SRP. CO (Hero) opens 2.5bb, BB calls. Flop Qs 9s 4d: BB check, CO bet 2bb, BB call. Turn Qs 9s 4d 6c: BB check, CO bet 7bb, BB call. River Qs 9s 4d 6c 2h: BB checks. Hero has Ks Ts (missed flush and straight draw). Pot ~24bb.
gto_explanation: Hero has zero showdown value and a polarized story: the bet-bet line repped a strong range and the river is a brick. A large bet pressures BB's check-call-check range, which is full of one-pair Qx and busted draws that cannot continue. The Ks is a useful blocker — it removes a chunk of the Ks-high spade flushes BB could have raised or called with. With no showdown value, checking back simply surrenders the pot; a near-pot bet maximizes fold equity against a capped, mostly-one-pair range.

description: 100bb. BTN opens 2.5bb, BB (Hero) calls. Flop 9h 8h 4c: BB check, BTN bet 2bb (~35% pot). Hero has Jh Th (open-ended straight draw plus flush draw).
gto_explanation: JhTh is a monster combo draw — an open-ender plus a flush draw plus a straight-flush card, often a coin flip or better against BTN's c-betting range. It is a premium semi-bluff check-raise: it builds the pot while equity is high, denies BTN's overcard and backdoor equity, and can barrel most turns credibly. Flat-calling under-realizes the hand's equity out of position; folding one of the strongest non-made hands in poker is a clear error.

description: 100bb. SRP. HJ opens 2.5bb, BB (Hero) calls. Flop Kc 8d 3s: BB check, HJ check. Turn Kc 8d 3s 5h: action on BB. Hero has 8c 7c (middle pair). Pot 6bb.
gto_explanation: When the preflop raiser checks back the flop their range is capped — they would c-bet most Kx and strong hands. That makes the turn a strong probe (lead) spot for the BB. Middle pair 87 is a fine merged probe: it gets called by worse pairs and gutshots and folds out the overcards HJ checked back, while still having a clear plan on most rivers. A medium ~50% size matches a merged value-and-protection range; an overbet would polarize too hard for a one-pair hand.
lesson_tag: Probe the capped range on the turn

description: 100bb. CO (Hero) opens 2.5bb, BTN calls. Flop Ts 6s 2c: CO check, BTN check. Turn Ts 6s 2c 7d: action on CO. Hero has Ac Jc. Pot 6.5bb.
gto_explanation: BTN's flop check caps their range — strong hands and most pairs would have bet when checked to. The turn is the moment to attack with ace-high hands like AJ that have overcard equity, a backdoor club draw, and blocker value, and that can fold out the many weak floats and underpairs BTN kept in. A moderate ~50% size is enough against a capped range; checking again simply concedes the pot to a range that mostly missed.

description: 100bb cash. Hero opens BTN 2.5bb with 7h 6h. BB 3-bets to 11bb. Action back on Hero.
gto_explanation: 76s against a BB 3-bet, in position, has the playability to continue: it flops straights, flushes, and strong draws, it is suited and connected so it realizes equity well even in a 3-bet pot, and it is getting a good price closing the action with position. 4-betting turns a hand with excellent implied odds into a bluff that must fold to a shove, wasting its post-flop value. Calling and playing position is the standard, highest-EV line.

description: 100bb. HJ opens 2.5bb, CO calls. Hero is in the BB with Ah Jh. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: AJs in the BB facing an open plus a cold-call is a strong squeeze. The flat-caller's range is capped — they would usually 3-bet their premiums — so squeezing applies pressure to two ranges that both struggle to continue, and AJs blocks AA/AK/AQ. Flatting invites a multiway pot out of position where AJs realizes equity poorly and is dominated by HJ's offsuit aces. The size is larger than a normal 3-bet to account for the extra dead money and the second player.

description: 100bb cash. Folded around to the SB (Hero), who holds Kd 9d. Only the BB remains. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: Modern SB-vs-BB strategy is predominantly raise-or-fold with only a small limping range, and K9s is firmly a raise. It is far too strong to fold heads-up against one random BB hand and it benefits from taking the betting initiative. Completing with K9s lets the BB realize equity for free and caps the SB's limp range. The larger 3.5x size is standard SB-vs-BB to charge the BB, who will be in position post-flop.

description: 100bb. Hero opens BTN 2.5bb with Qs Js, BB calls. Flop Qh Jd 9d: BB check, BTN bet 4bb, BB raises to 14bb. Hero has top two pair. Pot was 6.5bb.
gto_explanation: Top two is strong, but QJ9 with a flush draw is one of the most dynamic textures in poker. BB's check-raise range is polarized to made straights (KT, T8s), sets, two pair, and flush-draw and combo-draw semi-bluffs. Raising folds out the semi-bluffs Hero currently beats and gets called only by hands that have Hero crushed or are flipping. Calling keeps BB's bluffs and draws in, controls the pot, and lets Hero fold on the worst run-outs.
lesson_tag: Call top two vs c/r on wet board

description: 100bb. CO (Hero) opens 2.5bb with Ks Kh, BTN calls. Flop 9c 6c 3c (monotone). Pot 6.5bb. Action on Hero. Hero holds no club.
gto_explanation: On monotone flops the in-position caller holds many more flushes and single-club holdings than the preflop raiser, and the texture hits BTN's flatting range hard. KK with no club has no flush draw and is exposed. Solvers check a large share of the c-bettor's range on monotone boards — including most overpairs without a club — to control the pot and avoid being raised off equity. Betting bloats the pot out of position when any club turn is a disaster.

description: 100bb. Hero opens BTN 2.5bb with Ac 5c, BB calls. Flop Kd 8h 3s: BB check, BTN bet 2bb, BB call. Turn Kd 8h 3s 9h: BB check. Hero has Ac 5c (no pair, no draw).
gto_explanation: A5s c-bet the flop as part of a range bet, but the 9h turn improves a meaningful slice of BB's calling range (9x, new and existing heart draws) and A5c has no equity to draw to. Barreling turns a hand with occasional showdown value into a pure bluff with poor fold equity against the sticky Kx and 8x that called the flop. This is a textbook turn give-up: check back, retain the ace-high, and sometimes win unimproved or pick up equity on the river.
lesson_tag: Give up the turn with no equity

description: 100bb. SRP. CO opens 2.5bb, BB (Hero) calls. Flop 7h 6h 2c: BB check, CO bet 2bb, BB call. Turn 7h 6h 2c 5d: BB check, CO check. River 7h 6h 2c 5d 8s: action on BB. Hero has Tc 9c — the nut straight. Pot 12bb.
gto_explanation: Hero holds the nut straight and the river action leads into a CO range that checked back the turn — that range is capped, with few slow-played monsters. Against a capped range that still contains plenty of two pair, sets, and worse straights, the BB can overbet the nuts for maximum value, and Hero's range here also contains enough busted draws to balance overbet bluffs. Betting small with the stone-cold nuts simply leaves money on the table.
lesson_tag: Overbet the nuts vs capped range

description: 100bb. Hero opens MP 2.5bb with Ah Kd, BTN calls. Flop Ac 8c 5h: MP bet 4bb, BTN raises to 9bb (a small click-back raise). Hero has top pair top kicker.
gto_explanation: A small in-position raise on this texture is usually a merged, protection-oriented sizing — it contains flush draws, weaker Ax, sets, and two pair. AK is top pair top kicker, well ahead of the draws and worse aces in that range. 3-betting folds out everything AK beats and bloats the pot against the sliver that has Hero crushed (sets). Calling keeps the draws and worse aces in, controls the pot, and lets Hero evaluate turns. Folding TPTK to a tiny raise is far too weak.
lesson_tag: Call TPTK vs small flop raise

description: 100bb. UTG (Hero) opens 2.5bb with Qh Qd, MP calls, BTN calls. Flop Jh 6c 3d. Pot ~9bb, three-handed. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: In multiway pots ranges are stronger and the c-bettor should size down and bet less often — but QQ as an overpair on dry J63 rainbow wants to bet for value and protection against two opponents who each hold many Jx, draws, and underpairs. A smaller ~33% size charges the field, denies equity to overcards, and keeps the pot manageable out of position against two players. A large bet over-commits a one-pair hand multiway; checking gives two ranges a free card.

description: 100bb. Hero 3-bets from the BB over a BTN open; BTN calls. Flop 8c 7c 5d. Pot ~19bb. Hero checks, BTN bets 6bb. Hero has Ac Tc (nut flush draw plus a gutshot).
gto_explanation: AcTc on 875 with two clubs is a powerhouse semi-bluff in a 3-bet pot — the nut flush draw plus a gutshot to the nut straight, with an overcard. The low SPR means building the pot now with a check-raise both maximizes fold equity and sets up a profitable turn shove when called, and the Ac blocker removes a chunk of BTN's made flushes and flush draws. Calling is too passive with this much equity at a low SPR; folding discards one of the strongest non-made hands available.

description: Satellite-style final table: 4 players left, 3 seats paid, all stacks roughly equal at ~20bb. Folded to Hero on the BTN with Ah 9h. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: A9s is a comfortable chip-EV shove from the BTN at 20bb, but under heavy ICM pressure — a near-flat payout where survival matters far more than chip accumulation — marginal shoves that risk elimination become losing plays. With three of four paid and equal stacks, busting before another player forfeits a guaranteed pay jump. The ICM bubble factor inflates the cost of getting called and tightens the correct shoving range well below the chip-EV range, so A9s is a fold.

description: 100bb. Folded to the BTN, who opens 2.5bb. Hero is in the BB with Kc Js (offsuit). Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: KJo against a BTN open is comfortably within the BB's defending range and is primarily a call. It is too strong to fold getting a great price while closing the action, but 3-betting it as a bluff is poor: KJo unblocks much of BTN's calling range and is dominated by the value hands (AK, AJ, KQ) that continue. It also flats well — it makes strong top pairs and has straight potential. The correct mix is heavily weighted to calling.
lesson_tag: Flat offsuit broadways in the BB

description: 100bb. CO (Hero) opens 2.5bb with 5h 5s, BTN calls. Flop 9h 5d 2h: CO bet 3bb, BTN call. Turn 9h 5d 2h Kh (front-door flush completes). Pot ~13bb. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: Middle set is a monster, but the Kh both completes the front-door flush and is an overcard that smashes BTN's range — they hold many flushes (any two hearts that floated), Kx that just made top pair, and combo draws that got there. Checking middle set on this range-flipping card controls the pot, induces bluffs and thin value from BTN's now-confident hands, and avoids check-raises from the flushes. Betting into a range that just crushed the turn bloats the pot out of position while protecting very little.

description: 100bb. Hero opens CO 2.5bb with Ad Kc, BB calls. Flop 6s 5s 4d: BB leads (donks) 3bb into a 6bb pot. Hero has ace-king high with a backdoor flush draw.
gto_explanation: Low connected boards like 654 with a flush draw are exactly where a thinking BB builds a donk-leading range, and that range is condensed around made straights, two pair, sets, and strong draws — it is not bluff-heavy. AK is two overcards with only a backdoor flush and backdoor straight, not enough equity to continue against a strong, condensed donk range. Raising as a bluff runs into a range that rarely folds and often re-raises. Over-folding ace-high to a credible donk lead on this texture is correct.
lesson_tag: Respect the donk lead on low boards

description: 100bb. BTN (Hero) opens 2.5bb with Ah 5h, BB calls. Flop Kh 9h 4c: BB check, BTN bet 2bb, BB call. Turn Kh 9h 4c 7s: BB check, BTN check. River Kh 9h 4c 7s 2d: BB checks. Hero has Ah 5h — a missed flush, ace-high. Pot 9bb.
gto_explanation: It is tempting to bluff the busted flush draw, but the bluff you want to fire holds NO blocker to the hands you want to fold out. Ah5h holds the Ah — the nut-flush card — so it removes BB's missed-heart-draw bluff-catchers (the very hands that would fold) and blocks none of BB's Kx value. Ace-high also retains real showdown value against BB's busted draws. Solvers bluff the no-blocker busted draws here and check back the ace-of-hearts combos.
lesson_tag: Don't bluff with the wrong blockers

description: 100bb. Hero opens HJ 2.5bb with Ah Ks, BTN 3-bets to 9bb, Hero 4-bets to 21bb, BTN moves all-in for 100bb. Action on Hero.
gto_explanation: Once Hero 4-bets and faces an all-in 5-bet at 100bb, AK is priced in. Against a balanced 5-bet-shoving range — QQ+, AK, and a few bluffs such as A5s — AK has roughly 40%+ equity, and with the dead money already in the pot the pot odds make calling clearly profitable. Folding AK after 4-betting it is a large leak: AK sits at the top of the 4-bet value range and must continue against the shove.

description: 100bb. BTN (Hero) opens 2.5bb, BB calls. Flop As Kd 7c. BB checks. Hero has Kc Tc — second pair. Pot 5.5bb.
gto_explanation: AK7 rainbow is a textbook range-bet board for the BTN — Hero's preflop range connects with the ace far more than the BB's flat-calling range, so Hero can c-bet the entire range for a tiny ~25-30% size. Second pair KT belongs in that small-bet range: it wants thin value from worse Kx and underpairs, denies equity to the overcard and gutshot combos, and a small size keeps Hero's bluffs cheap. A large polarizing bet is unnecessary on such a static board where the whole range bets.
lesson_tag: Tiny range bet on dry ace-high

description: 100bb. Hero opens CO 2.5bb with Ad Jc, BB calls. Flop Js 8d 3c: BB check, CO bet 3bb, BB call. Turn Js 8d 3c Qh: BB check. Hero has top pair, decent kicker. Pot ~12bb.
gto_explanation: AJ flopped top pair, but the Qh turn is an overcard that improves BB's range — Qx that floated, QJ, and the many gutshots and draws that picked up equity — and does not improve AJ. Betting for value gets called mostly by better (Qx, two pair, sets) and folds out worse. Checking back controls the pot, keeps BB's bluffs in for a river bluff-catch, realizes Hero's showdown equity, and avoids being check-raised off a one-pair hand.
lesson_tag: Pot-control top pair on scare turns

description: 100bb. Hero opens BTN 2.5bb with Js Jd. BB 3-bets to 11bb. Action back on Hero.
gto_explanation: JJ in position against a BB 3-bet plays best as a call. 4-betting JJ bloats the pot and gets it in well only against a narrow QQ+/AK value range while folding out everything JJ dominates, and it turns the hand face-up. Flatting keeps BB's entire 3-bet range — bluffs and worse pairs included — in the pot, retains position, and lets Hero realize JJ's equity across all run-outs. Folding a hand this strong is far too tight.

description: 100bb. CO opens 2.5bb, BB (Hero) calls. Flop Td 7s 4c: BB check, CO bet 2bb, BB call. Turn Td 7s 4c 5h: BB check, CO bet 6bb, BB call. River Td 7s 4c 5h 6d: BB check, CO bets 10bb. Hero has 9c 8c — the nut straight. Pot was 19bb.
gto_explanation: 98 makes 6-7-8-9-T, the nut straight, and no better hand is possible. CO has barreled three streets, repping a value-heavy range of sets, two pair, and overpairs that will pay a raise while drawing dead. Just calling with the absolute nuts leaves a large amount of value uncollected against a range that has shown this much strength. The raise size targets a call from CO's strong one-pair-plus hands; folding the nuts is unthinkable.
lesson_tag: Raise the river with the nuts
